entries.title: Iran war: US conducts strikes for 9th night in a row
entries.summary: The US has launched another wave of attacks against Iran, this time reportedly hitting targets in the northwestern city of Tabriz. DW has more.
entries.content:
Iran

Iran war: US conducts strikes for 9th night in a row

Published July 20, 2026last updated July 20, 2026

The US has launched another wave of attacks against Iran, this time reportedly hitting targets in the northwestern city of Tabriz. DW has more.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • The US attacks Iranfor the ninth consecutive night since a ceasefire collapsed
  • USmilitary says strikes aimed to degrade Tehran's ability to target commercial and civilian shipping in theStrait of Hormuz
  • Iranian media report strikes hitting northwestern city of Tabriz

Below is a roundup of the main developments from the US-Iran war on July 20, 2026.

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Vessel on fire in Strait of Hormuz, UK maritime agency says

Vessel on fire in Strait of Hormuz, UK maritime agency says

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations ‌agency ⁠said that a vessel is on fire ‌8 nautical miles (14.8 kilometers) northwest ‌of Oman's ‌Kumzar in the Strait of Hormuz. 

"UKMTO has received a report of an incident 8NM northwest of Kumzar, Oman. UKMTO have received information from military authorities that a vessel is on fire," the British organization said in a statement.

It added that the ​cause ‌of the fire ​had ⁠not been verified.

 

Skip next section US military member killed in Iraq during 'controlled demolition' of Iranian drone

US military member killed in Iraq during 'controlled demolition' of Iranian drone

A US service member was killed in Iraq during a controlled demolition of an Iranian drone, the US military said late Sunday night.

The service member is the third American to be killed in recent days, after two US troops were killed in Jordan on Friday in a strike claimed by Iran's Revolutionary Guards.

A third service member was missing after that strike. The US military said Sunday that "unidentified remains" had been found and were being examined.

At least 17 US military personnel have been killed since the war began in late February.

Skip next section US strikes Iran for 9th consecutive night

US strikes Iran for 9th consecutive night

The US military launched a new wave of strikes targeting Iran early on Monday, the ninth consecutive night of attacks.

"The strikes will continue degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners transiting the Strait of Hormuz," the US military's Central Command (CENTCOM) said in a post on X.

Iranian state media reported that the northwestern city of Tabriz was struck. Previous waves of US attacks in the latest escalation had mainly targeted sites along the Persian Gulf coast in southern Iran.

The US has resumed daily attacks on Iran since a ceasefire collapsed on July 8.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

The United States military has launched another round of strikes targeting Iran.

The strikes are the ninth night in a row that the US has attacked Iran nights since US President Donald Trump declared on July 8 that the shaky ceasefire between the two countries was "over".

Stay tuned as we bring you the latest on the US war against Iran and other developments from the Middle East.

entries.title: Spain win World Cup for second time after beating Argentina 1-0
entries.summary: After a 16 year wait, Spain have claimed a second men's World Cup. Ferran Torres scored the winner in a deserved 1-0 victory over Argentina, which means Spain are now men’s and women’s world champions.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

After a 16 year wait, Spain have claimed a second men's World Cup. Ferran Torres scored the winner in a deserved 1-0 victory over Argentina, which means Spain are now men’s and women’s world champions.

Patience proved the critical virtue for Spain. They had to wait for a kickoff delayed by — among other things — a Tom Cruise speech. They had to wait twice as long as usual at half time. And they had to wait for 106 minutes of play and their 20th shot before Ferran Torres lashed home the goal that would win them a second men's World Cup.

"I’m very moved. Looking back and thinking about them, we’ve won everything, absolutely everything, with this generation of players," said Spain coach Luis de la Fuente. "The match should have been decided much earlier ... but this is a World Cup final and you have to dig deep even against ten men, and we’re prepared for anything."

Spain's captain and player of the tournament, Rodri, lifted the trophy. But not before Donald Trump made his presence known. The US president was booed when he entered the pitch with FIFA president, Gianni Infantino and appeared keen to stay for the trophy lift, just as he had in the Club World Cup final last year. Rodri appeared to subtly attempt to usher Trump off the stage before Infantino jogged over to join Trump on the sidelines of the celebrations.

Messi on the margins

For much of Sunday’s final, that moment had felt purely a matter of time, as the European champions thoroughly outclassed their South American counterparts.

Just as they did in their impressive semifinal display against France, Spain dominated the ball early and their teenage superstar, Lamine Yamal, came close to opening the scoring in the fifth minute. Seconds later, at the other end of the pitch and the age scale, Lionel Messi was beaten to a through ball by Spanish keeper Unai Simon. It was as close as Messi, 39, got to a significant impact on the game, which may well be his last at a World Cup.

"They were better, to be honest," Messi said postmatch. “We lost the game, and we accept that. That doesn’t mean that we will forget everything we’ve done so far, so I’d like to thank my people, my players and the country."

As the first half wore on in the New York New Jersey stadium, Spain dominated the ball but Argentina held firm, with a smart Emi Martinez save from Spanish striker, Mikel Oyarzabal, as close as either side came before the elongated interval.

Half-time show keeps players waiting

For the first time in World Cup history, a Superbowl style half time show was on the cards. Performers including Madonna, Justin Bieber and BTS took to the pitch with the break extended — contrary to both football convention and FIFA laws — to almost double the standard 15 minutes.

It wasn’t enough to change the pattern of the game. Spain continued to pass, Argentina continued to scrap, hassle and rely on Martinez to keep Spanish shots at bay.

A red card for midfielder Enzo Fernandez in injury time made Argentina’s task even tougher as the final headed into extra time, as it now has in five of the last six finals. Argentina tired but somehow clung on, with Spain repeatedly wasteful.

Spain keep composure

Surely the South Americans, who had failed to take a single shot in the entire game, couldn’t somehow win this?

But Spain have already proved they won’t panic, with late winners in two of their knockout matches. Indeed, the final bore some resemblance to their tournament opener — a 0-0 draw with Cape Verde. After that, Spain slipped somewhat under the radar for a time, with a lack of cutting edge thought to count them out.

But as the tournament progressed, Luis de la Fuente’s team have delivered a masterclass in control while sharpening their attacking weapons to win a first World Cup since 2010. Spain’s women are also reigning world champions, winning the tournament in 2023.

"I think that in the end, the goal belonged to 47 million people, not to me or the 26 players here," said the goalscorer, Torres. "I believe that today destiny was written, it was meant for us to win, far from our fans, but we tried to make them feel as close ‌as possible."

World Cup overshadowed by Iran war, Balogun controversy

The final ended a tournament that has offered much from a sporting perspective: the heroics of Cape Verde, the race between Messi and Kylian Mbappe for the tournament — and overall World Cup — top scorer and shocks such as Germany’s exit to Paraguay and Norway toppling Brazil.

But politics has also proved impossible to ignore.

The barring of Somali referee Omar Artan from entering the US added to the sense this was not a World Cup for the whole world. Iran’s treatment, being forced to fly in and out of games and given less preparation time as a result, was a reminder of the conflict between the US and Iran. 

Donald Trump’s intervention in the suspension of a red card ban for US striker Folarin Balogun also  amplified already loud questions about FIFA corruption.

But, ultimately, the tournament was decided in the same way it always has been. And, on Sunday in New Jersey it was settled, once again, in favor of Spain.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

entries.title: New Bundesliga TV deal in US prioritizes reach over money
entries.summary: The Bundesliga has a new home on American screens after Germany's top football league left ESPN. The new deal is a sign of the times.
entries.content:
July 19, 2026

The Bundesliga has a new home on American screens after Germany's top football league left ESPN. The new deal is a sign of the times.

After six years on ESPN, the Bundesliga has a new home in the United States of America.

A combination of USA Network and Fandango has reportedly struck a deal through the 2030-31 season to be the English-language broadcaster of Germany's top flight in the US. The deal is reportedly worth $20 million (€17.4 million) per year, which is noticeably less than the $34 million per year paid by ESPN.

USA and Fandango are part of Versant, a media company that is trying to develop itself as a home for broadcasting rights below the top leagues of the NFL and NBA. Fandango is predominately an app to buy movie tickets, but Versant is looking to diversify its offering by including live sports. All 300-plus Bundesliga games will air on either channel, with USA requiring a subscription and Fandango being free and ad-supported.

The reaction from US-based Bundesliga fans on social media was mostly negative.

"This will only make the Bundesliga even more obscure," posted @skierpro on X. "Any momentum the league had in the US and growing viewership will be destroyed. The folks in Germany signing this agreement have no idea about the US it seems. They just shot themselves in the foot with this deal."

"This is bad. The simplicity of having every game on ESPN that already has a lot of other sports was perfect. Fandango, a company with nearly no sports streaming experience, may be free, but the games on USA will require an expensive YT TV, Fubo or similar package," posted Corbin Williams on X.

"SAD! USA is OK but Fandango is not a popular streaming app. ESPN was easily accessible. Games on Fandango is going to be like going to the bike shop to buy a BMW," wrote Eric Smith on social media.

New Bundesliga deal a case of less money, but more reach

Some believe the drop in financial return is a sign of the times for the Bundesliga. Others see it as an opportunity.

"From the outside, the new agreement appears to deliver less media-rights income per season but broader distribution, greater discoverability, and a better chance of reaching viewers beyond the Bundesliga's existing fan base. In essence, the reported financial return appears lower, but the potential reach is greater," Dominik Schreyer, a professor of sports economics at Germany's Otto Beisheim School of Management, told DW.

While Schreyer admits that the reported fee being less than the previous deal is disappointing from a business perspective, it is also important to consider its context.

"The DFL is facing a difficult market reality. The media-rights market has become more selective, while competition for audience attention is intensifying. Maximizing the check from each individual cycle may therefore no longer be the only sensible objective," Schreyer said.

"The new distribution model could still prove strategically valuable if its broader reach helps build a larger and more commercially attractive audience over time," he added.

ESPN's coverage in terms of reporters and commentators was praised by most fans, but the new deal suggests that perhaps it wasn't the ideal place for Germany's top flight.

"My best guess is that the previous model provided insufficient prominence within ESPN's crowded sports portfolio, potentially limiting audience growth. The Bundesliga may now be more important to a partner seeking to establish and differentiate its own live-sports offering in an intensely competitive market, although we cannot know from the outside which considerations ultimately drove the change," Schreyer said.

Is the Bundesliga really growing in America?

"The 2026 World Cup has highlighted the tremendous growth potential of soccer in the US," Robin Austermann, Bundesliga Americas executive vice president, said in a statement. "We've seen that momentum firsthand, with the number of Bundesliga fans in the US growing by 43% over the past five years."

While that may be true, German football's struggle more generally cannot be helping the Bundesliga's position in the market. Bayern Munich have long been dominant domestically, removing both the feeling of competition and suspense in the league. The number of star players isn't high compared to other leagues, and Germany's poor showing at recent World Cups has also weakened the standing of German football globally.

It's clear that the Bundesliga faces an enormous task when it comes to improving its position at the negotiating table for broadcasting deals, much of which is beyond its control. Nevertheless, there is hope that, as mentioned by Austermann, the football momentum provided by the 2026 World Cup in the US, Canada and Mexico can be harnessed to the Bundesliga's advantage.

"It now needs to use prominent scheduling, strong promotion, and localized storytelling to build consumption capital," said Schreyer. "Once viewers discover the product, repeated exposure helps them learn about the clubs, recognize the stars, understand the rivalries and gradually turn occasional viewing into habit, fandom, and ultimately commercial value."

Edited by: Chuck Penfold

entries.title: Algeria seeks larger EU energy role amid regional tensions
entries.summary: Algeria and Germany agreed to deepen energy ties during President Abdelmadjid Tebboune's recent visit to Berlin. But Algiers is still grappling with tensions at home, over Western Sahara and instability in the Sahel.
entries.content:
Algeria

Algeria seeks larger EU energy role amid regional tensions

July 19, 2026

Algeria and Germany agreed to deepen energy ties during President Abdelmadjid Tebboune's recent visit to Berlin. But Algiers is still grappling with tensions at home, over Western Sahara and instability in the Sahel.

For Algerian President Abdelmadjid Tebboune, this week's visit to Berlin marked his first official trip to Germany since he was elected president in 2019. The two-day visit was highlighted in a joint statement with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, in which the two leaders emphasized "the quality of cooperation" between their countries and pledged to deepen long-term energy cooperation.

"Algeria is a central partner for Europe's energy security. Natural gas supplies and cooperation on renewable energies are to be expanded. In the long term, it is about establishing a southern hydrogen corridor — from North Africa to Germany," Cerstin Gammelin, the spokesperson for German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier posted on X on Thursday.

Algeria is home to Africa's second-largest proven natural gas reserves after Nigeria. It is also Africa's largest natural gas producer. On July 2, Algeria's state-owned energy company Sonatrach delivered the first shipment of liquefied natural gas (LNG) directly to Germany's Wilhelmshaven 1 floating LNG import terminal. A direct pipeline to Germany would eliminate the need for liquefaction, LNG shipping and regasification for those volumes. This could reduce transport costs for gas deliveries, as is already the case with Algeria's two existing pipelines to Italy and Spain.

"The war in Iran and the disruption of oil exports via the Strait of Hormuz have reinforced Algeria's energy importance," said Alice Gower, director of geopolitics and security at the London-based consulting firm Azure Strategy.

"Although Algeria cannot replace Gulf supplies, its pipeline gas reaches southern Europe without passing through Gulf shipping lanes, offering European governments supply diversification at a time of renewed concern about energy security," she said.

This view is echoed by Hafed Al-Ghwell, director of the North Africa, Mediterranean and the Sahel program at the Washington-based think tank Stimson Center. Al-Ghwell told DW he recently met with Algeria's foreign minister and members of the cabinet.

"You can sense very clearly that there is the political will at the highest level of government that Algeria needs to take advantage of what is happening globally as well as its unique position as a major gas producer," he said.

Algeria at key strategic crossroads

Algeria's value for Europe extends well beyond gas, Gower pointed out. "Algiers offers cooperation on counterterrorism, migration and Mediterranean stability," she told DW.

For years, Algeria has cooperated with European countries on migration management, including returns, border issues and combating smuggling networks.

Moreover, Algiers holds a distinctive diplomatic standing, maintaining relations with both Iran and the United States, as well as the Gulf countries, China and Russia. It is also located at a key strategic crossroads between North Africa and the Sahel region.

"President Tebboune has also placed renewed emphasis on African cooperation, reflecting Algeria's ambition to act as a bridge between North Africa, the Sahel and the wider continent," said Gower.

However, she also noted that this approach is a response to pressure. Algeria is increasingly isolated on the issue of Morocco's claim over the disputed Western Sahara territory. While Algeria supports the local Polisario Front in their quest for independence, Morocco considers Western Sahara part of its territory.

After US President Donald Trump recognized Morocco's sovereignty over the region in 2020, Spain, France and several African nations followed suit.

"Algiers' outreach also aims to preserve its regional influence amid growing Moroccan initiatives," observers wrote in a March analysis of the Institute for Security Studies.

The authors also highlighted that Algeria is a pivotal actor in the Sahel as it shares more than 2,280 kilometers (1,420 miles) of porous borders with Mali and Niger, and faces many of the same challenges, including terrorism, cross-border crime and irregular migration. "Reopening channels of dialogue would serve pressing security, economic and political interests for all."

Algiers has recently sought to repair ties with Mali and Niger. It also supports a political solution for its third large neighboring country, Libya, which remains de facto split in two administrative parts.

Domestic pressure

"A fundamental principle in political science is that foreign policy often reflects domestic politics," Toufik Bougaada, professor of political science at the University of Algiers, told DW.

In his view, this is true for Algeria's situation as well. The Stimson Center's Hafed Al-Ghwell also noted that Algeria is now able to leverage its current stability to address internal dynamics.

"Private sector investment from abroad could help create jobs and specifically address the high level of youth unemployment," he explained.

Algeria has not yet published an official 2026 youth unemployment rate. The latest available World Bank estimate puts youth unemployment at around 29–30%.

Moreover, after the country's longtime ruler Abdelaziz Bouteflika resigned following the Hirak pro-democracy protest movement in 2019, many of the movement's demands for greater political openness remain unmet. Algeria's political system remains controversial until this day.

According to the Bertelsmann Transformation Index 2026, hopes for reform in Algeria after the 2019 Hirak protests have largely faded, with the country having "largely reverted to pre-Arab Spring conditions." The Washington-based think tank Freedom House again classified Algeria as "Not Free" in its Freedom in the World 2026 report.

Recent assessments by Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International describe Algeria as experiencing a continued contraction of civic space. HRW noted that the authorities continued prosecuting journalists, activists and online critics.

Gower therefore remains cautious. "Algeria's expanding geopolitical value has altered the balance of international engagement," she said, adding that "this gives Algeria greater scope to pursue its domestic agenda with reduced external pushback."

Edited by Rainer Sollich

entries.title: Germany news: Merz moots Cabinet reshuffle after Spahn exit
entries.summary: Chancellor Merz has said he might use the forced resignation of a senior ally as a chance to reshuffle his Cabinet. Meanwhile, a train security guard was critically injured in a clash with a passenger.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany news: Merz moots Cabinet reshuffle after Spahn exit

Published July 18, 2026last updated July 20, 2026

Chancellor Merz has said he might use the forced resignation of a senior ally as a chance to reshuffle his Cabinet. Meanwhile, a train security guard was critically injured in a clash with a passenger.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Freidrich Merz says Jens Spahn's resignation "could be an opportunity" to reshuffle his Cabinet and rotate personnel
  • Senior conservative politicians are set to meet to start discussing how to replace Spahn on Monday
  • German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt has said there is an increased security threat, with attacks possible "at any time"
  • Dobrindt cited an increased volume of intelligence reports, with possible targets including infrastructure, individuals and institutions
  • A Deutsche Bahn railway security guard has been critically injured after falling from a moving train during a confrontation with a passenger
  • Jürgen Klopp says he's reached a deal with Red Bull that could lead to him being Germany's next national team coach

This blog is now closed. Thank you for reading.

Here is a roundup of of the latest headlines from Germany on Saturday, July 18 and Sunday, July 19:

Skip next section Bavaria's Markus Söder reportedly pelted with tomato at local event

Bavaria's Markus Söder reportedly pelted with tomato at local event

Bavaria's state premier, Markus Söder, visited celebrations in Bernau am Chiemsee on Sunday, praising rural areas as the "soul of Bavaria." 

Söder said in a post online that he had always enjoyed visiting the area, and pledged to develop "tourism, natural protection and the needs of locals in a balanced manner in future."

The conservative CSU leader made no mention, however, of reports on newspaper Bild's website and later on German news agency DPA of a man jumping out a bush and throwing a tomato at him amid the visit. 

Bild cited eyewitnesses and a video of the incident. It said that the presumed attacker was swiftly apprehended and that Söder was unhurt.

Bernau am Chiemsee is a small municipality of around 7,000 people on the Chiemsee lake, southeast of Munich and near the Austrian border.

Skip next section Parookaville dance music festival wraps up in Weeze

Parookaville dance music festival wraps up in Weeze

Germany's largest electronic music festival Parookaville concluded on Sunday night on the Dutch border in Weeze. 

After three days of music performances and five days of camping on site, organizers and police both issued positive initial appraisals.

A total of roughly 225,000 people attended across the entire event.

Fesitval co-founder Bernd Dicks, who got a taste for the scene during his years reporting from similar events, said, "We're not just the biggest festival in North Rhine-Westphalia and one of the biggest in Germany — we're also one of the most peaceful."

He said that figures for criminality and misbehavior were "absolutely below average."

Police in nearby Kleve were also largely positive, saying there had been assaults, sexual harassment cases and theft, "but nothing too serious, thank God."

Police spokesman Stefan Sparberg told regional public broadcaster WDR that it was a very pleasant event for law enforcement.

He said there had also been drug offenses, but said this was "not our main issue at this event." Since the partial legalization of cannabis in Germany in 2024, he said, "people smoke dope here, that's generally understood, the event's over-18s only."

The local supermarket in Weeze, a small town of just over 12,000 people, reported a busy week. By late Saturday, it had sold 12,500 turkey pastries, 15,400 bananas, 222,000 beers, 40,000 packs of ice cubes and 35,400 frozen pizzas.

NRW's State Premier Hendrik Wüst also visited on Friday, sporting shades and some grey-blue Adidas summer shoes, touring the festival with Bernd Dicks.

Skip next section German pundit Christoph Kramer slams pitch quality for World Cup final

German pundit Christoph Kramer slams pitch quality for World Cup final

German World Cup winner turned pundit for the ZDF public broadcaster Christoph Kramer was in typically pithy form on seeing the pitch for the final in Rutherford, New Jersey this evening.

Kramer was less than complimentary of the groundskeeping staff, who have been battling searing July temperatures and frequent use of the MetLife Stadium pitch amid the tournament.

"It's like a skittles alley with a carpet laid over it. For Spain in particular, it's a different kind of football match," Kramer said, insinuating that Spain's possession-based passing game could be compromised by the imperfect playing surface.

Kramer is no stranger to World Cup finals involving Argentina, but famously his memory of his personal experience is not what it should be.

The midfielder suffered a concussion in Germany's win after extra time against Messi's Argentina 12 years ago, later saying he had no recollection of the biggest match of his life.

Skip next section Klopp says he's reached a 'generous' deal with Red Bull

Klopp says he's reached a 'generous' deal with Red Bull

Jürgen Klopp has said that a key hurdle to him becoming the Geman national team's next coach has been cleared.

In the build-up to the World Cup final on MagentaTV, where he has been appearing as a pundit, Klopp gave an update on his talks with the managing director of Red Bull, Oliver Mintzlaff, on the sidelines of the final in the New York area.

"I have reached an agreement with Red Bull — a generous arrangement," Klopp said.

Klopp is currently Red Bull's "Head of Global Soccer" and contracted until 2029, a post he took up after leaving Liverpool and saying he would take a timeout from top-level coaching.

Asked about a final agreement with Germany's football association, the DFB, Klopp said: "Nothing is final yet, but the talks are ongoing and everything is moving in the right direction."

Based on reports and off-the-record comments, the DFB made clear to Red Bull that it could not pay a large severance package for Klopp. However, reports of "creative solutions" to the impasse, such as Klopp continuing to operate in some commerical capacity on behalf of the energy drinks giant, soon circulated.

Skip next section Rhine river levels show signs of recovery after extreme heat

Rhine river levels show signs of recovery after extreme heat

Measuring stations are still reporting low water levels at most posts along the Rhine river in western Germany. 

Water levels along Europe's busiest inland freight waterway, connecting Rotterdam's vast port to much of Germany, are roughly half what you'd expect at this time or year, or around a third of the annual averages. This is a result of two recent heat waves, the earlier of the two bringing unprecedented temperatures to parts of Germany.

Low summer water levels can cause complications for freight companies, sometimes forcing them to run barges with only partial loads, particularly if they are carrying high-density cargo.

The levels have however risen slightly in the past few days — particularly in major cities in North Rhine-Westphalia like Bonn, Cologne and Düsseldorf — and are forecast to continue to improve as the week begins.

Düsseldorf's city website predicts that levels in the city, which is one of the river's shallower and busier points, should roughly double by Tuesday when compared to Friday evening's low point.

Although not much rain is forecast in the NRW region in the coming days, areas upstream have improved levels that should soon be reflected further north.

Skip next section Munich enthusiasts dance in the dawn at English Garden ball

Munich enthusiasts dance in the dawn at English Garden ball

Folk dance enthusiasts or simply early-morning revellers gathered in central Munich for the annual Kocherlball on Sunday.

A crowd of some 12,000 people gathered at the Chinese Tower dining room and beer garden in the English Garden park in the Bavarian capital.

People arrived to reserve tables as early as 3 a.m. for the tradition to ring in the dawn by candlelight; every table was taken by 4 in the morning, Bavaria's BR broadcaster reported.

The tradition traces its roots back to 1794, when an English count Benjamin Thomspon threw a ball for staff members at a military workhouse he'd established in the city.

The tradition caught on and endured annually for decades, weather permitting at least.

Many participants worked as serving girls or maids or soldiers, or as cooks in the mess hall — hence the Bavarian dialect name Kocherlball. They had to gather at dawn because they were expected back on duty later in the day.

The event was banned in 1904 amid reports of rowdy behavior. But in 1989, the tradition was revived, by this point more for enthusiasts from the general public than serving staff.

Skip next section Klopp-Mintzlaff talks in US eagerly awaited by Germany fans

Klopp-Mintzlaff talks in US eagerly awaited by Germany fans

It may be World Cup final night, but disappointed Germany fans also have their eyes peeled for news on the next national team coach.

Decorated former Mainz, Dortmund and Liverpool coach Jürgen Klopp is seen as the front-runner and the German football association or DFB's target to replace Julian Nagelsmann. 

Klopp is in the US working with the MagentaTV broadcaster during the World Cup.

And on Sunday, the managing director of Klopp's primary employer, Oliver Mintzlaff of Red Bull, is also expected in the New York area for the big game.

Klopp and Mintzlaff are expected to be meeting, seeking a resolution to Klopp's current contractual status as Red Bull's "Head of Global Soccer."

German media appear to have jumped the gun slightly on Sunday, with both Sky and Bild am Sonntag reporting that an agreement had been reached paving the way for Klopp to join the Germany setup.

But later in the day, Klopp's advisor Marc Kosicke told the SID sports news agency that no such deal had yet been struck.

The DFB, meanwhile, said it was not commenting on ongoing negotiations until they were completed.

Skip next section A45 Autobahn bridge in Sauerland demolished at second attempt

A45 Autobahn bridge in Sauerland demolished at second attempt

It took two blasts to get the job done, but one half the A45 highway bridge over the valley in Ottfingen in Sauerland has been demolished.

The operation began first thing on Sunday morning and was only completed around six hours later, when preparations were made to demolish 12 stilts and a part of the bridge platform that did not collapse in the first controlled explosion.

"Now the entire half of the construction is lying precisely where it's meant to," demolitions master Stefan Grässle said after the secondary blast.

People keen to watch the demolition could only do so from beyond a 300-meter (roughly 1,000-foot) security cordon.

Traffic was diverted off the A45 Autobahn amid Sunday's operation.

The demolished 235-meter bridge, opened in 1971, is one of a pair across the valley, typically with one for traffic in each direction. Its deteriorating condition made demolition necessary.

The other bridge will remain standing, with two lanes of traffic traversing it in each direction, while a replacement is built. Currently it's scheduled for completion by 2030.

A host of highway bridges in the populous western state of North Rhine-Westphalia — which is also a trunk route for pan-European freight traffic — are in poor condition and need of renovation.

This issue has come to the fore in DW's neighborhood in Bonn of late, where the main highway bridge over the Rhine river was found to be damaged on June 3 and now faces a complete closure that looks set to last at least two years, severely impacting traffic.

Skip next section Germany seeing a 'crisis of trust in democracy' — Merz

Germany seeing a 'crisis of trust in democracy' — Merz

Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Sunday that Germany is going through a "crisis of trust in democracy," citing the tone of debate in parliament, the Bundestag.

Merz told broadcaster ZDF it was "not just the task of a government but also the task of all democratic parties" to remedy the problem, saying the atmosphere in parliament was "not an advertisement for democracy."

The chancellor said he "went home in a very concerned state" after the last session before the summer break.

"You can be of a different opinion on an issue," he said. "And I was also twice leader of the opposition, and didn't give either of the governments any quarter."

But the current "personal denigration" by almost all parties was damaging democracy, he said.

"We are used to it from the AfD and now from the Left Party too, but also now from the Greens," he said. "It was all the same sound."

During the last plenary session before the summer, parliamentarians passed a health care reform that opposition parties had partly tried to stop in court.

A controversial new law setting out rules for heating systems was also passed.

Skip next section Cabinet reshuffle possible after Spahn resignation — Merz

Cabinet reshuffle possible after Spahn resignation — Merz

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Sunday that he would nominate a proposed successor for Jens Spahn as parliamentary leader of his conservative bloc "relatively soon" after consulting with Christian Social Union (CSU) leader Markus Söder.

"The parliamentary party is, if necessary, functional. We will talk about this in party committees over the next few days and we will make a proposal relatively soon, but we are not pressed for time now," he told public-service broadcaster ZDF.

He did not rule out reshuffling the Cabinet in the process.

"It could be an opportunity," he said, when asked whether politicians from the government could take on the job of parliamentary leader.

The question came amid speculation as to possible replacements for Spahn, who resigned from the position on Saturday amid controversy of his use of surrogacy to have a child.

Both Merz and Spahn are members of the Christian Democratic Union (CDU), which explicitly rejects surrogacy.

Among the names that media have mentioned as possibilities are chancellery head Thorsten Frei, Health Minister Nina Warken (both CDU) and Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt (CSU). 

Merz said that all parliamentary parties were now on their summer break and that there would be no parliamentary sessions until September.

Skip next section Solar industry threatened by Economy Ministry plans — association

Solar industry threatened by Economy Ministry plans — association

Plans by the Economy Ministry to cut subsidies for small solar systems from 2027 could hit investments and put thousands of jobs at risk, the German Solar Association (BSW) has warned.

Under plans published on Friday evening for a new version of the Renewable Energy Act (EEG) and the Grid Package, the feed-in tariffs from solar systems of up to 25 kilowatts installed capacity would be guaranteed for only up to 36 months instead of a current 20 years.

After this, operators of such systems would have to switch to direct marketing, selling their electricity on power exchanges via a service provider, where prices fluctuate and are not predictable.

Economy Minister Katharina Reiche of Chancellor Friedrich Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) has said the subsidies are no longer necessary as  homeowners can get their return on investment purely through self-consumption of the energy they produce.

BSW chief executive Carsten Körnig said the plans were "completely out of date."

"They keep private households dependent on fossil energy for longer and put tens of thousands of jobs in the solar industry at risk," he said.

The German Renewable Energy Federation (BEE) also warned about the plans, with President Ursula Heinen-Esser saying, "Instead of reliable investment conditions, the federal government is creating new uncertainty."

Skip next section Court rejects arrest warrant for suspect in violent train incident

Court rejects arrest warrant for suspect in violent train incident

A district court has rejected a prosecutor's arrest warrant for a suspect in a violent clash on a train that left a security guard critically injured, meaning that the man was set free on Saturday evening.

The 36-year-old suspect, who is known to the criminal justice system for violent offenses and was free on probation, is alleged to have become involved in a confrontation that led to the 26-year-old guard falling from a moving train.

It was not immediately clear why the arrest warrant application, based on suspicion of grievous bodily harm, was rejected.

A spokesman for the public prosecutor's office said the security officer reportedly fell against the train door during a scuffle with a passenger, who was reportedly drunk, during a ticket inspection, causing the door to tear from its hinges,

The train from Offenburg to Karlsruhe in southwestern Germany was traveling at 120 kmh/75 mph when the guard fell through the open door, he said.

In February, a train conductor died of injuries sustained in a confrontation with a passenger during a ticket inspection in a case that shocked the country. 

Skip next section IN DEPTH: CDU's Jen Spahn steps down amid surrogacy controversy

IN DEPTH: CDU's Jen Spahn steps down amid surrogacy controversy

The parliamentary leader of Chancellor Friedrich Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU), Jens Spahn, has resigned after he and his husband decided to have a child via surrogacy despite Spahn previously voting on party lines to oppose the practice.

Spahn has anyway long been at the focus of several controversies, as you can read in our article: Germany: CDU's Jens Spahn resigns amid surrogacy controversy

Skip next section Tesla looks to step up production in Germany

Tesla looks to step up production in Germany

Tesla's German manufacturing unit plans to increase production at its factory in Grünheide near Berlin, according to an annual company report for 2025.

The report said recovering demand and improving profits were behind the plans to significantly increase production volume.

However, Tesla said the expansion still depended on economic conditions, warning that the outlook could be affected by geopolitical factions and potential supply chain disruptions.

Earlier this year, Tesla said it wanted to increase output at the Grünheide plant — its only European production site — to as many as 7,500 vehicles a week, or about 375,000 a year, as well as expanding battery cell production.

The factory opened in March 2022 and currently produces the Model Y. At the end of 2025, it employed about 11,000 people including temporary workers, the report said.

In 2025, vehicle production fell to about 202,000 units from around 211,000 the previous year, a drop that the company said was due mainly to a model changeover and the introduction of new variants.

However, the German unit reported a net profit of €77.1 million ($88.2 million) for 2025, up by about €20 million from the previous year,

Skip next section Welcome back to our coverage

Welcome back to our coverage

The DW newsroom in Bonn wishes all users guten Tag as we continue to keep you up to date with happenings in Germany.

If you have not yet caught up with the latest major story from German politics — the resignation of CDU parliamentary group leader Jens Spahn — you have your chance here.

We will also be looking at automotive giant Tesla's plans to step up its production volume in Germany and follow up on an incident on a train that left a security guard critically injured.

Stay with us for these stories and more.

entries.title: Spain: Emergency crews battle wildfire north of Madrid
entries.summary: More than 25 firefighting planes have been deployed to battle the blaze in central Spain, which has been raging for three days.
entries.content:
July 19, 2026

More than 25 firefighting planes have been deployed to battle the blaze in central Spain, which has been raging for three days.

Firefighters battled to contain a fast-spreading wildfire north of Spain's  capital, Madrid, on Sunday.

The country is still reeling from one of the deadliest wildfires in modern times that killed 13 people and destroyed 7,000 hectares in southern Almeria province earlier this month.

What do we know?

The latest fire started on Thursday in central Spain's Guadalajara province, which includes the Sierra Norte Natural Park.

The area is described as a forest-covered, mountainous region that is home to endangered species, including eagles and wolves.

El Pais newspaper reported that the fire had burned through more than 13,000 hectares by Sunday.

Public broadcaster RTVE reported that the flames had advanced a further 14 kilometers (9 miles) overnight.

More than 500 firefighters and emergency personnel have been deployed to contain the blaze.

Ground crews are also being assisted by 25 firefighting aircraft, officials said.

Fire crews save 'many lives'

The head of the Castilla-La Mancha regional government, Emiliano Garcia-Page, paid tribute to emergency workers in a post on X, saying they had "managed to save many lives."

Several hundred residents were ordered to evacuate the area.

No casualties have been reported so far.

Man held over suspected arson

A man, accused of starting the fire, was arrested on Thursday, police said, citing a tipoff from neighbors.

Spain remains on high alert for summer wildfires as a severe drought and low humidity affect much of the country at the height of the tourism season.

A major wildfire earlier this month in Almeria province, in the southern Andalucia region, left 13 people dead, including several foreign nationals.

The country remains in shock after revelations that several of the victims were found in burnt-out vehicles, having failed to flee the flames.

Zaragoza fire burns for 4 days

Another wildfire in the northeastern Zaragoza province has been burning since Wednesday, forcing the evacuation of more than a thousand residents.

The regional government warned on Friday evening that the blaze is "far from under control."

However, the head of the Aragon regional government, Jorge Azcon, said Saturday was the "first day we can breathe a sigh of relief," as the fire had been mostly contained.

Since the beginning of the year, wildfires have destroyed more than 80,000 hectares across Spain.

According to the European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS), that's nearly double the area burned during the same period last year.

Scientists say human-caused climate change is making heatwaves longer, hotter and more frequent — drying out landscapes and fueling more destructive wildfires.

Edited by: Saim Dušan Inayatullah

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred news source on Google by clicking here, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Fact check: Spotting AI-generated 'historical' images
entries.summary: DW Fact check selected three "historical" videos that aren't real — they're AI. Could you recognize the fake? Here's how to spot it — and why it matters.
entries.content:
TechnologyGlobal issues

Fact check: Spotting AI-generated 'historical' images

July 19, 2026

DW Fact check selected three "historical" videos that aren't real — they're AI. Could you recognize the fake? Here's how to spot it — and why it matters.

In Italy's Pompeii, archaeologists have, for the first time, used artificial intelligence to reconstruct the image of a victim of the volcanic eruption in 79 C.E.

AI is increasingly being used to generate "historical" images as the technology has become widely available, mostly without the consultation of experts. Roland Meyer, a media scholar at the University of Zurich, thinks the trend is no coincidence.

"Through AI, it seems possible to recombine images of the past, generate synthetic images that do not exist but perhaps could have existed, and thus fill perceived or actual gaps in the archive and in the historical record," he said. "To me, however, this seems like a deceptive promise."

AI generators have been known to make mistakes and "hallucinate." DW Fact check explains how to spot AI-created "historical" videos.

#1: Could this video even exist?

Many viral AI‑generated "historical" videos that claim to show the past blur the line between fact and entertainment. They can often be identified by simply asking whether the video could have been made.

"Are we talking about a time when videos already existed, or are we talking about a time when they didn't?" asked historian and AI-specialist Sebastian Kubon.

This video supposedly depicting Pompeii can be discredited on that basis alone.  

It also shows clear signs of AI generation. People move in a zombie-like manner, and the woman's appearance looks far more modern than what we know of ancient Rome.

The rise of AI tools has made it easier than ever to create such "historical" images and videos, but experts warn that AI is trained on existing data and will repeat its biases.

"The images of the past generated by AI primarily reproduce the typical patterns, cliches and stereotypes with which that past has been depicted thus far," said Meyer.

"Above all, these are images of our expectations: not a window into the past, but a mirror of our present."

#2: Look for visual clues and check the source

Secondly, a close look at the images themselves and their sources is helpful.

In this video, the camera glides in closer to a group of soldiers holding the US flag, showing their faces as they stare into the distance. It looks more like a Hollywood film than authentic documentary footage.  

One soldier in the background glitches unnaturally. The watermark also gives it away: this video was produced by Veo, a generative AI tool.

The video mentioned above is based on the iconic 1945 photograph of US soldiers on Iwo Jima. There is no authentic video footage of this specific moment.

All the experts DW spoke with emphasized the importance of critical source analysis. Where does the image come from, who presents it, for whom and why?

The video's account promises to explore "patriotic stories" and "educational and entertaining content that brings America's story to life." It reveals a handful of similar AI-generated videos. 

Images and videos always present a particular perspective, Kubon pointed out.

"They involve a selection of image fragments and audio sources; in essence, they present one perspective on the story. We're too quick to assume that just because something was recorded, it's somehow the truth."

Jürgen Zimmerer, an Africa history professor at the University of Hamburg, warned that visual sources still carry a great deal of authority and have been problematic even before AI.

"Images have always been highly controversial sources. This is evident from the fact that dictatorial regimes, whether Nazi Germany or Stalinist Russia, have also retouched images," he said.  

The difference today is the scale at which AI can generate altered and completely fabricated scenes.

#3: Compare with verified historical sources

Look to archives, museums, reputable media or academic experts.

A video showing alleged CCTV footage of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster in 1986 is also AI-generated. Whereas we can find the authentic photograph of Iwo Jima in the US National Archives, no authentic footage exists from this moment in Chernobyl. 

Such AI creations raise the question of responsibility.

In comparison to the use of fictional scenes in documentaries, where a director or production team decide how to portray the past, AI pulls from large datasets without understanding of historical context.

"It produces something that looks plausible at first glance, not because it is historically accurate, but because it matches our expectations," Meyer explained.

This is most dangerous when authentic footage does exist, and the video could be real.

Whereas most viewers understand that videos from ancient Rome are fake, large numbers of AI-generated images threaten to replace real documentation of times the technology was present.

Zimmerer emphasized that history is always an approximation. "We approach historical events when there is no original film footage, and when you pretend there is, you make something seem clear that is not actually clear," he said.

So, don't fall for AI-generated historical slop. Dig deeper instead and explore the real past.

entries.title: EU ban on destroying unsold clothes takes effect
entries.summary: Under new rules to reduce waste, large companies across the European Union can no longer dispose of unsold clothes, accessories and footwear.
entries.content:
July 19, 2026

Under new rules to reduce waste, large companies across the European Union can no longer dispose of unsold clothes, accessories and footwear.

A ban on the disposal of unsold clothes and shoes took effect across the European Union on Sunday, in the bloc's latest move to cut waste.

Hundreds of thousands of tons of fashionwear are destroyed every year across the 27-member EU due to damage, old stock and returned online orders.

The new rule is expected to affect large fashion retailers, wholesalers and manufacturers of apparel — only 20% of which are produced in the EU.

What are the new rules?

Under the EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation, large companies with more than 250 employees and over €50 million in net annual turnover are banned from destroying stocks of unsold clothing, accessories and footwear.

Firms must now find ways of selling the products, including via discounts, alternative markets and charity donations.

Unsold clothes can only be destroyed when items are unsafe, damaged, counterfeit or rejected by charities, according to a post on the European Commission's website.

Companies are now required to publish annual reports on the goods they have discarded and keep records for five years.

The regulation, which was approved by Brussels more than two years ago, will be extended to medium-sized firms in 2030.

How big is the fashion waste issue?

Concerns about so-called fast fashion — low-cost, lower-quality mass-produced clothing — have grown significantly over the past decade.

According to the European Environment Agency (EEA), around 4% to 9% of unsold textile products are destroyed each year.

The waste has been exacerbated by rapid growth in online shopping and returns.

Across the EU, one in five fashion goods ordered online is later returned to the retailer and not resold.

Combined, this amounts to hundreds of thousands of tons of clothes, accessories and footwear.

The new ban aims to help reduce the impact on the environment, as the incineration of millions of clothes hits efforts to cut greenhouse gas emissions.

EU states are also under pressure to consider waste from raw materials, water, energy and transport in the apparel sector and to encourage more recycling and the reuse of products.

In a recent LinkedIn post, the EEA said the bloc wanted the textile industry to become more sustainable.

"A circular economy — where products are designed to last, and are reused, repaired and recycled — is no longer just a vision, but is being embedded in law through these new legislative measures," the EEA wrote.

The fashion industry has repeatedly defended its tactics, noting that disposal is often cheaper than storing, repairing or discounting the products.

What's the reaction to the ban?

The German Retail Federation (HDE) said consumers would benefit from the ban as more discounted apparel is put on sale through outlets, clearance markets and second-hand channels.

HDE Managing Director Stefan Genth warned, however, that the rule change would be a challenge for many stores.

"Not all unsold goods can be resold or donated easily," he said, due to damaged packaging, high logistics costs, lack of demand or low product values.

Edited by: Saim Dušan Inayatullah

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred news source on Google by clicking here, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: China firm seeks damages over state control of British Steel
entries.summary: The UK government took over British Steel last year to prevent the closure of the country's last primary steelmaking facility. China's Jingye Group, which bought the firm in 2020, claims it is owed compensation.
entries.content:
July 19, 2026

The UK government took over British Steel last year to prevent the closure of the country's last primary steelmaking facility. China's Jingye Group, which bought the firm in 2020, claims it is owed compensation.

A Chinese steel giant demanded compensation from the United Kingdom  government on Sunday, claiming the state's takeover of its subsidiary, British Steel, was "outright robbery."

The UK government took operational control of British Steel last year, after owner Jingye Group warned that Britain's last factory capable of making steel from scratch was no longer financially viable.

What did Jingye say?

In a statement on social media, Jingye threatened legal action against the UK government, accusing ministers of "trampling on international investment rules."

Describing the nationalization as "outright robbery," the Chinese firm said it "reserves all legal rights, firmly defends its lawful rights and interests, and will pursue full compensation through legal means to the very end."

"The UK disregarded Jingye's continuous investment and significant contribution and was only willing to provide almost zero compensation,” the statement continued.

The company noted that nationalization could cost UK taxpayers more than 1.5 billion pounds ($2.02 billion, €1.76 billion) by 2028.

Jingye said it had begun a procedure under bilateral investment agreements to seek damages, without expanding further.

China's Foreign Ministry backed the compensation move, calling on the UK to "earnestly respect market principles and the spirit of contract, and find solutions on compensation and other issues acceptable to both sides."

Why did the UK seize control of British Steel?

After Jingye last year planned to close the blast furnaces at its plant in Scunthorpe, in northern England, the UK government passed emergency measures, allowing it to take operational control of British Steel.

The immediate concern was that the blast furnaces would be shut permanently, ending the UK's capacity to produce virgin or primary steel from raw materials. The plant accounts for the vast majority of British Steel's operations.

The government cited national security for the move, which came five years after Jingye bought British Steel from a London-based private equity firm for around 70 million pounds.

Ministers cited the need to protect strategic steel supplies for construction, rail infrastructure and defense.

The move was also seen as a way to protect thousands of jobs in a deprived area of the UK. The Scunthorpe plant currently employs about 2,700 people.

In recent weeks, the UK Parliament has passed legislation that allowed the government on Thursday to bring British Steel into full public ownership.

Under the new law, an independent valuer must assess whether any compensation is payable to Jingye.

British Steel: Swinging from state and market control

The UK steel industry was nationalized after World War II, including the facilities at Scunthorpe, which have been producing steel since the late 19th century.

The sector returned to private hands in 1988 under the late Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher and has since changed hands several times due to massive losses stemming from high operating costs, fierce competition and overcapacity.

The British Steel brand was revived in 2016 by Greybull Capital, which bought the firm for 1 pound and briefly turned a profit.

But after the firm later filed for insolvency, it was acquired in 2020 by China’s Jingye Group.

Despite an investment of 1.2 billion pounds reported by Jingye, the company said the Scunthorpe plant was still losing around 700,000 pounds per day.

Edited by Richard Connor

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred news source on Google by clicking here, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Russian army deserters in Germany: 'No choice but to run'
entries.summary: Almost all asylum applications by Russian deserters are being denied by German authorities. DW and Russian-language media outlet Astra spoke to one of the young men who fears he'll be sent back to die on the front lines.
entries.content:
Germany

Russian army deserters in Germany: 'No choice but to run'

July 19, 2026

Almost all asylum applications by Russian deserters are being denied by German authorities. DW and Russian-language media outlet Astra spoke to one of the young men who fears he'll be sent back to die on the front lines.

Nikita Zvezdov was just 15 years old when Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. He was drafted into the Russian military straight after his 18th birthday in April 2024. Shortly before that, he had been failed at a vocational school he was attending in Krasnoyarsk and he believes that his expulsion was deliberate.

Over the past few years there have been reports that Russian students were not only drafted into the army but also prevented from passing their exams or deliberately flunked, so their conscription could not be hindered. This information has not been verified.

In May 2024, after receiving his conscription notice, Zvezdov was assigned to military unit 25573 near Ussuriysk. Soldiers are frequently killed serving this unit and there have also been reports of harassment, extortion and beatings. On various internet forums, the unit is well known for its criminality.

Zvezdov said officers in the unit pressured him and other conscripts to commit to a longer period of service in the army. "I promised myself and my relatives that I would not sign a contract for the [conflict]," Zvezdov said, adding that he had never wanted to fight against Ukraine in the first place.

"They sent me to a training ground where they also did whatever they wanted — officers shot many conscripts in their legs, beat them up and punished them for everything they could and could not do," Zvezdov continued. "Do 200 pushups in a gas mask and run 50 laps in a bulletproof vest with 25 to 30 kilos on top."

Zvezdov said he started having suicidal thoughts and even tried to jump off a cliff, but was prevented from doing so by officers. After that, another officer forced him to sign a service contract.

The young man is not alone in this experience. The Russian military is experiencing personnel shortages and conscripts are regularly pressured to join for longer.

The mothers of a number of Russian soldiers told the Russian media outlet Astra, which operates mostly via its Telegram channel and which has been designated a "foreign agent "by the Russian government, that their sons were deceived or harassed until they signed contracts.

'No choice but to run'

A month after signing his contract, Zvezdov was told he was being sent to units operating in Russian-occupied areas of Ukraine, near Melitopol, which were well-known for a high rate of casualties. "I realized that in principle I definitely have no choice but to run. You won't be able to wait it out either," he explained.

After Zvezdov got his first pay as a soldier — 40,000 Russian rubles (€450/$510) — the young man applied for leave, ostensibly in order to stock up on provisions before he left for the front. A former convict, who'd also been conscripted, was assigned to monitor him.

Zvezdov was supposed to check in with him via WhatsApp every few hours. But he was lucky — his minder didn't pay all that much attention to the job. Zvezdov threw away his uniform and fled to Armenia.

He wasn't actually classified as a deserter for another six months. By then Zvezdov had traveled further, first to Bosnia and Herzegovina, then onto Croatia, where he applied for political asylum.

But without waiting for a decision on his asylum application, the young Russian then moved on again, arriving in Germany in December 2025, where his grandfather lives. In Germany, he applied for asylum once again.

The German authorities rejected his application, citing the European Union's so-called Dublin rule, which means that the country where an asylum-seeker enters is the state responsible for their application. In Zvezdov's case, this is Croatia.

Germany was supposed to send Zvezdov back to Croatia within six months; the deadline for that would be at the end of July 2026. If he is not sent back, responsibility for his asylum application returns to Germany.

However, according to human rights activists, Russian deserters have almost no chance of getting approved for asylum in Germany. That is despite the fact that the last German government, which was more politically left, had said in 2022 that conscientious objectors and deserters deserved protection.

But public statements made by politicians don't make any difference to interpretations of the law, explained Alexey Kozlov, manager of human rights and research projects at Solidarus in Berlin, an organization that supports civil society in former Soviet Union states. Many similar applications have been rejected based on the rationale that deserters do not face a "real risk" of severe persecution or deployment to Ukraine upon return.

The Federal Office for Migration and Refugees, or BAMF, confirmed this. According to BAMF, every asylum application and the applicant's risk of persecution is investigated on an individual basis.

Protection is granted only "if there is a real, individual and justified fear of persecution," BAMF stated.

Improvised prisons and torture chambers

In fact, there are hundreds of reports of Russian deserters being sent directly to the front lines and mistreated. Since the beginning of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, media outlet Astra has identified 29 illegal detention centers for Russian conscientious objectors, mostly in the Russian-occupied Ukrainian regions of Luhansk and Donetsk but also on Russian territory.

The improvised prisons are set up in basements, abandoned industrial buildings, camps, private homes and other structures. Thousands of Russian soldiers and Ukrainian citizens, including wounded and disabled individuals, have been held there. In the prisons, officers extort money from prisoners, urinate on them, starve them, beat, torture or even kill them.

According to Astra, the Russian military has set up something akin to a "torture chamber "in the Petrivka quarry in Donetsk.

"They bring the soldiers there with bags over their heads," a former Russian soldier told Astra on condition of anonymity; he escaped from his unit in 2024. "They don't feed them. If you want to go to the bathroom, they give you a bag and tell you to go. They hung us from the ceiling by our hands and feet."

The former deputy platoon leader was imprisoned there along with the men under his command because they refused to go to the front line. He said almost all his men were killed there.

According to figures from the Germany's parliament, the Bundestag, a total of 126 people were deported back to Russia in 2025. Solidarus' Kozlov said since Germany's new coalition government took office a year ago, the already-slim chances of asylum for Russian deserters are now practically zero.

Church asylum delays deportation

As for Zvezdov, after he was denied asylum in Germany he managed to get what is known as "church asylum."

Church asylum — or "Kirchenasyl" in German — happens when a congregation agrees to temporarily shelter refugees facing deportation. There is no legal right to church asylum in Germany but in practice, if a church grants somebody asylum, the state will often pause deportation proceedings and review the case.

Because of this, Zvezdov was able to move from a refugee camp to a church near Aachen, in the western state of North Rhine-Westphalia. There, he spends most of the day working in the kitchen or grounds; the move has allowed him to remain in Germany for now.

Back in the camp where he had had previously been living, the German police came to collect him on the night of June 16. Had he been there, he would likely have been deported to Croatia the next day.

Now the young Russian is counting the hours until July 30, when the responsibility for his asylum application will fall back to German authorities.

But should they reject his application, Zvezdov isn't sure what he will do — most likely try to find refuge in a country outside of the EU, he said. He's quite sure he won't be able to go back to Russia for decades.

This article was originally written in Russian.
